text: sentiments) were established as anthropological foundations. This is the overall claim that this book wishes to pursue, most directly in Part II, tracing this centralization and permanentization of liminality in a variety of directions, referring first and foremost to cultural and political developments. Chapter Structure This book is divided into two parts. Each part is composed of four chapters that form a whole. Part I is introductory and constitutes an attempt to situate Arnold van Gennep and the concept of liminity within its proper historical and disciplinary context. It is an engagement with intellectual history, but for a purpose. This part provides a proper and thorough introduction to the concept of liminality and discusses how it developed within anthropology in the early twentieth century, and later within the social and human sciences from the 1960s until today. It thus seeks to establish a more solid ground for applying liminality as an analytical tool of relevance for the social sciences writ large. Any book on liminality must depart from anthropology: it was via anthropology that liminality entered our conceptual vocabulary, and it could not be otherwise. Chapters 1 and 2 of this book will set the frame by discussing how the concept of liminality emerged and developed via the work of Arnold van Gennep. While it is well-known that Arnold van Gennep came up with the concept of liminality Into Liminality 15 in his book Rites of Passage, subsequent scholarship has completely bypassed how this discovery was part of a larger intellectual project that van Gennep was trying to establish in the early twentieth century. This is the focus of Chapter 1. Before van Gennep eventually turned to the exclusive study of folklore (and this happened in the early 1920s), this academic project developed in sometimes open competition with Emile Durkheim. Chapter 2 will discuss this largely unknown intellectual battle and explain why van Gennep’s project regrettably drowned in oblivion while Durkheim’s functionalist approach largely triumphed. Sociologists and anthropologists with a specific interest in intellectual history can read this chapter on its own. Chapter 3 will proceed to describe how the concept of liminality re-emerged in the 1960s, initially via the writings of Victor Turner. The chapter will present Turner’s development of liminality, indicating how liminality must be tied to an experientially based process-approach. However, the chapter will end on a series of warning notes already alluded to: namely that we should not simply follow Turner’s suggestion that liminality in our modern world has turned into the ‘liminoid’. This is certainly the suggestion that made Victor Turner famous well beyond anthropology, and made him a reference figure in a diversity of study areas, from theatre to literature to tourism studies. We argue instead that any application of liminality must, as a minimum, stay close to one aspect of its original meaning as defined by van Gennep: namely, that is has to do with the passing of a threshold and therefore with transition. If it is not about transition, it is not about liminality. If adopting Turner’s notion of the liminoid in the analysis of liminal-like phenomena in modern, consumerist society does not represent a convincing analytical strategy, Chapter 4 suggests ways in which the concept of liminality can indeed serve to highlight dynamics of thought, experience and social change in both ‘simple’ and less simple social situations. This involves a careful consideration of how a concept derived from ritual passages in small-scale societies can be applied to social and political change in larger social complexes, or even entire civilizational settings. It is suggested that Turner’s study of pilgrimage may in fact represent a more promising development. Here Turner indicated how a liminal state may become ‘fixed’, referring to a situation in which the suspended character of social life takes on a more permanent character. This was much in line with what Turner himself had suggested earlier, namely that in the monastic and mendicant states of the world religions, transition had become a permanent condition. This point – again, the ‘permanentization’ of liminal conditions – will be taken up in subsequent chapters. Chapter 4 thus stands midway between Parts I and II, seeking to reconstruct and redirect Victor Turner’s project, also referring back to some of Turner’s less discussed last writings. The central argument proposed in this chapter, drawing here extensively on the analysis offered by Arpad Szakolczai, is also that Turner’s recognition of the proximity of his own project with that of the German philosopher Dilthey was correct and important. The chapter will discuss the implications of that statement, and those implications will serve to identify a framework for thinking with liminality which relates to transition and change Liminality and the Modern 16 both at the individual and societal levels. Concerning the connections between the experience of liminality and modern thought, the impact of the Lisbon earthquake on Kant’s thinking will be invoked as a particularly salient example. The chapters that make up Part II are in different ways attempts to apply this framework to specific practices or historical situations. They each engage a particular aspect of liminality within a horizon of modernity. Chapter 5 addresses the larger time period that followed the ‘waning of the Middle Ages’ (Huizinga) and the collapse of the Renaissance to the mid seventeenth century, the period often referred to as ‘early modernity’. This historical period, following here Huizinga and Voegelin, was not experienced by contemporaries as a ‘breakthrough’ or a passage toward a more mature modernity, but rather as a total collapse of order, an age of anxiety, an ‘age of great confusion’. The age was in desperate search for new ordering principles within politics, philosophy and science. Two cases will briefly be invoked: the life experiences and related thought systems of the contemporaries Thomas Hobbes and René Descartes. It will be argued that the disorder of the early modern age was overcome with the ideational and political-institutional structure that was established with the ‘moment of Westphalia’, and with Cartesian science laying the foundations for
